macro_segments.title: The laundering of blood-stained garments and the removal of tumah when the garment leaves the Azarah

micro_segments.title: The basic case: a garment with chatas blood that left the Azarah and must be brought back for laundering
When a blood-stained garment becomes tamei outside the Azarah: tearing it to remove tumah status while retaining garment identity

macro_segments.title: Pottery and metal vessels used for cooking: removal of tumah and preparation for continued use

micro_segments.title: Pottery vessels that absorbed chatas: making a hole to remove tumah status while retaining vessel functionality
Metal vessels and the process of scouring and rinsing to cleanse them after cooking

macro_segments.title: The laundering of blood-stained garments and the removal of tumah when the garment leaves the Azarah (continued)

micro_segments.title: The Gemara's challenge to tearing: if it stops being a garment, there is no mitzvah to launder it
The resolution: leaving enough material to retain garment status while removing tumah through partial separation
Rav Huna's position: the degree of tearing required and the rabbinic vs. biblical status of retained tuma

macro_segments.title: Pottery and metal vessels used for cooking: removal of tumah and preparation for continued use (continued)

micro_segments.title: The Gemara's parallel analysis: vessels need to lose their original identity while retaining enough status for the purification process
Metal vessels refashioned after being damaged: whether the mitzvah applies to a newly fashioned vessel

macro_segments.title: The me'il of the kohen gadol: laundering without tearing when the garment becomes tamei

micro_segments.title: The prohibition against tearing the me'il and the alternative method of partial immersion for laundering
Bringing sections of less than three by three finger-widths into the Azarah without violating the restriction on tamei objects
The conceptual framework: treating partial entry as if the piece had been separated, so it would not retain tumah status
Rav Adda bar Ahava's challenge: thick and soft garments should allow larger sections to be laundered at once
The resolution: the measure of three by three applies because the garment remains whole and connected

macro_segments.title: The seven cleansing agents used for laundering and the problem of urine in the Azarah

micro_segments.title: The requirement of seven cleansing agents applied sequentially with rubbing, including urine
The problem: urine cannot be brought into the Azarah, yet it is necessary for effective laundering
The solution: mixing urine with spit to make it acceptable for use in the Azarah
Reish Lakish's view: adding spit with each cleansing agent is the normal procedure
Rambam's disagreement: rejecting the use of urine entirely in the laundering process

macro_segments.title: Cooking chatas and other kodashim in pottery and metal vessels: the scope of the requirement and types of korbanot affected

micro_segments.title: Whether cooking or merely pouring hot liquid into a vessel triggers the requirement for cleansing
The dispute over whether the mitzvah applies to kodashim kalim or only to kodshei kodashim
The gemara's derivation: even pouring hot chatas into a vessel requires cleansing of that vessel

macro_segments.title: The broader halachic framework of irui and cooking: absorption vs. cooking and their relationship

micro_segments.title: Rami bar Chama's question: does an oven require cleansing if the meat is only roasted on a spit without touching the walls
The distinction between cooking (bishul) and absorption (blia): cooking in a pot requires both, absorption alone suffices
Rava's proof from the case of irui: absorption without cooking is sufficient, showing that cooking alone may not require cleansing
The Gemara's evidence from the metal oven of the Mikdash: choosing metal to avoid the problem of cooking chatas in pottery

macro_segments.title: Ovens smeared with fat and the status of bread baked in them: the problem of fleishig bread and methods of koshering

micro_segments.title: Rabba bar Ashi's ruling: an oven smeared with animal fat becomes permanently fleishig and cannot be used for bread
The concern about hergel aveira: bread as a staple will inevitably be eaten with dairy or meat products
The braita's requirement: koshering pottery ovens by burning them out from the inside is possible
The distinction between ovens and pots: ovens can be koshered with internal fire, pots cannot for practical reasons
The concern about protecting pottery vessels: even though burning inside could theoretically kasher pots, we do not permit this method

macro_segments.title: The fundamental question: why must pottery vessels of chatas be destroyed rather than koshered like ovens

micro_segments.title: The apparent contradiction: if ovens can be koshered, why must chatas pots be destroyed?

topical_tags.term: Laundering in holy place (lekabbes bimkom kadosh)
Tearing the garment (karo'o) to remove tumah
Metal vessels (kli nechoshet) and scouring/rinsing (merika ushtifa)
Three by three (shalosh al shalosh) measurement
Kli rishon (primary vessel) vs. kli sheni (secondary vessel)
Oven vs. pot distinction in koshering
Rav Adda bar Ahava on thick garments
Reish Lakish on spit with cleansing agents
Rami bar Chama on oven cooking
Rabba bar Ashi on fleishig oven
